moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with one of the most flexible opening moves in chess, controlling the crucial central squares e5 and d4 without immediately occupying them. This move is the hallmark of the Zukertort Opening, named after the 19th-century master who favored this patient, positional approach—it allows us to adapt to Black's setup rather than committing to a rigid pawn structure. By placing our knight on f3 before moving pawns to the center, we keep our options open for either a kingside fianchetto, a d4 advance, or other strategic ideas depending on how Black responds.

Black immediately signals their intention to fianchetto the kingside bishop on g7, preparing a flexible setup that controls the long diagonal and the central d4 square. This move is typical in modern chess—it's less committal than playing e5 or d5, and it allows Black to see how we develop before deciding on a central strategy. The move is perfectly sound, though it does create a slight weakness on the light squares around Black's king, something we'll want to keep in mind as the opening continues.
